caveats: a: The BLS occupation mix is for all electrical equipment, appliance, and component manufacturing.
a: AI exposure is separated from conventional panel assembly automation, though machine vision and digital engineering overlap.
rho: Manufacturing-wide AI evidence is not specific to switchgear.
rho: Large firms have more structured product data and integration capacity than many target firms.
e: The frozen count is a margin bridge, not a verified list of acquisition targets.
e: Establishment counts include locations belonging to larger strategic manufacturers.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed deals.
s5: Strategic purchases of product lines or plants may not be qualifying firm transfers.

q: Material content and supply-chain pricing can dominate contract economics.

o: Consolidation toward large OEMs can displace LMM suppliers without removing the need for a manufacturer.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Configured and custom electrical equipment is sold through purchase orders and project contracts, often fixed-price and long-cycle, with repeat revenue from expansions, spare parts, replacement breakers, upgrades, commissioning, and installed-base support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, pure distributors or field contractors without qualifying manufacturing, and relay or industrial-control manufacturers classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers supplying switchgear, switchboards, distribution panels, power circuit breakers, fuses, bus duct, and related engineered assemblies, replacement equipment, commissioning, or repeat support to external utility, data-center, industrial, commercial, and infrastructure customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 335 occupation mix, not wage-weighted task exposure in 335313. I map electrical assembly, fabrication, wiring, testing, and material handling to low substitution, with higher exposure in engineering-document retrieval, configuration, estimating, drafting support, scheduling, procurement, inspection triage, order processing, and administration. The proxy may understate engineering intensity in custom switchgear or overstate exposure where safety-critical review remains mandatory.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix is for all electrical equipment, appliance, and component manufacturing.
AI exposure is separated from conventional panel assembly automation, though machine vision and digital engineering overlap.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupational wage matrix joined to task-level time studies from custom and standard switchgear plants, including engineering, wiring, assembly, testing, quality, planning, and administration, would materially refine exposure.
a.rationale: Electrical and electronic assemblers and other production roles remain prominent, but custom-engineered equipment also carries substantial design, configuration, quoting, planning, test-document, and project-management work that AI can assist.

d5.bridge: Powell and Eaton are large, diversified suppliers rather than US LMM 335313 firms. Powell reported $1.4 billion of September 2025 backlog and $1.6 billion by December, while Eaton reported 29% year-over-year Electrical-sector backlog growth and 16% rolling-order growth in Electrical Americas. I temper those nominal, market-share-sensitive indicators into a still-strong five-year constant-price quantity ratio. The proxy may overstate growth if large suppliers capture megaprojects disproportionately or understate subcontract and regional capacity needs.
caveats: Backlog and orders are nominal and affected by price, mix, acquisitions, lead times, and market share.
The evidence is dominated by large public manufacturers.
d5.change_evidence: NEMA or Census constant-dollar six-digit shipments and orders, segmented by utility, data center, commercial, industrial, oil and gas, replacement, and service, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Data centers, grid modernization, electrification, utility investment, reshoring, LNG, and industrial megaprojects create strong equipment demand, while replacement breakers and installed-base upgrades add resilience. Project timing, interest rates, utility budgets, capacity additions, and technology changes constrain the upper range.

caveats: The frozen count is a margin bridge, not a verified list of acquisition targets.
Establishment counts include locations belonging to larger strategic manufacturers.
e.change_evidence: A verified review of the 105 dataset-estimated LMM firms covering ownership, manufacturing content, certifications, customer concentration, engineering depth, captive status, and installed-base revenue would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Many regional custom panel, switchboard, retrofit, and replacement-equipment manufacturers can be independent merchant firms with transferable engineering teams and customer approvals. Captive shops, multinational plants, pure distributors, owner-dependent engineering businesses, and firms lacking durable certifications reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 335313 ($3,956,494,619) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 335313 ($14,653,850,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $421,233 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 335313); class <5: 108 firms, avg revenue/firm $671,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $131,772 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 55 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,235,273 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $438,561 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 61 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,583,475 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $703,078 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 105 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,280,886 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,194,310 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $61,905,211 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,145,802 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $318,059,125 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,403,200 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 105

caveats: Solid-state protection and integrated power electronics may change product architecture.
Consolidation toward large OEMs can displace LMM suppliers without removing the need for a manufacturer.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and product roadmaps measuring standardized modular procurement, direct digital configuration, utility self-manufacture, solid-state substitution, and consolidation into integrated OEM platforms would materially change operator requirement.
o.rationale: Customers will still require an accountable manufacturer for physical current-interruption, protection, distribution, enclosure, wiring, testing, certification, and commissioning. Digital configuration and smarter controls alter the equipment and workflow but do not eliminate the operator producing it.

q.bridge: Powell Industries is much larger than the lens firms, but its disclosure that revenue is primarily generated from custom-engineered products under long-term fixed-price contracts reveals a relevant contracting mechanism. I infer that productivity gains can be retained through a contract and partly into capacity-constrained quoting, while future bids, customer negotiation, and competition share gains. The proxy may overstate retention if LMM firms have less differentiation or understate it where niche certifications create scarcity.
caveats: Powell emphasizes large custom projects and may not represent standard panelboards or fuses.
Material content and supply-chain pricing can dominate contract economics.
q.change_evidence: LMM contracts and bid histories showing fixed-price versus indexed terms, escalation clauses, change orders, rebid frequency, backlog pricing, win-rate elasticity, and realized post-productivity margins would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-price custom work, engineering content, qualifications, lead-time scarcity, and installed-base compatibility support retention. Rebid cycles, sophisticated procurement, material escalation, customer concentration, and competitors' capacity additions erode some benefit over five years.

caveats: Manufacturing-wide AI evidence is not specific to switchgear.
Large firms have more structured product data and integration capacity than many target firms.
rho.change_evidence: Audited LMM deployments showing pre/post engineering and production hours, quote accuracy, design errors, test escapes, rework, lead time, and customer or certification acceptance would materially move implementation.
rho.rationale: Configuration, quote, design retrieval, scheduling, procurement, documentation, and test-analysis workflows are implementable within five years. Product-safety liability, customer approvals, standards compliance, legacy engineering data, supply constraints, and accountable design and test release limit autonomous deployment.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans across US employer-business owners, with 22% reporting such plans. I shade the base slightly downward for the gap between intention and completed qualifying control transfers, while allowing an upper range for aging owners and strong strategic demand. This may overstate completion or understate unsolicited acquisitions.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed deals.
Strategic purchases of product lines or plants may not be qualifying firm transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed private 335313 control transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, electrical-equipment advisers, and trade publications, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Succession pressure supports regular opportunities, and strong end-market demand can attract strategic buyers. Customer approvals, working capital, certification, founder engineering dependence, and buyer diligence still prevent some planned exits from closing.

principal_driver: Sustained grid, data-center, electrification, and replacement investment supports high operator-required demand.
principal_weakness: Safety-critical physical work and customer-approved engineering limit substitution, while public-company demand proxies may overstate LMM participation.
